coredata.dc:title: The use of crude carbon dots as novel antioxidants for natural rubber
coredata.dc:creator: Huangfu, Shanshan
Jin, Guangzhi
Sun, Qingwei
Li, Liwei
Yu, Peng
Wang, Runguo
Zhang, Liqun
coredata.dc:description: Natural rubber (NR) is acknowledged to be a strategically important material, which spans multiple applications from tires to shock absorbers, generally, the antioxidant is indispensable for NR to prolong the service life and improve the long-term reliability. Recent reports demonstrated that the purified carbon dots with the capability of scavenging oxy radicals can be effective antioxidants for rubbers. However, the application of purified CDs is greatly restricted by the multiple tedious, complicated, time-consuming, and expensive post-purification processes. Herein, we first synthesize the amine-passivated crude carbon dots (CCDs) by a facile microwave-assisted pyrolysis method, and we attempt to directly use the CCDs (without any purifications) as a novel and inexpensive antioxidant for NR. To achieve a fine CCDs dispersion within NR matrix, we disperse the CCDs into the NR matrix by the liquid feeding and in-situ compatibilizing. The CCDs fluid was firstly prepared by mixing the CCDs with water, then the CCDs fluid together with the compatibilizer was melt-compounded with NR. The results showed that the well-dispersed CCDs endowed NR with the impressive antioxidative property. Overall, this work extends application scope of CCDs and provides unique inspiration to develop novel and effective antioxidants for elastomers.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Crude carbon dots
Natural rubber
Liquid feeding
Anti-oxidation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CCD fluid and rubber composites
Dispersion of CCDs within the rubber
Aging performance of the NR composite

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FTIR spectrum for CCDs. (b, c, d) XPS spectra for CCDs, (b) survey XPS spectra, (c) N 1s XPS spectra, (d) C 1s XPS spectra. (e) UV-Vis spectra for CCDs. (f) Fluorescence emission spectra of the CCDs with different excitation wavelengths ranging from 300 to 440 nm. (g, h) TEM image for DETA-CCDs. (i) The CCDs fluid exhibits Tyndall effect.

Time dependence of UV−vis absorption spectra for DPPH radical (0.1352 mM) upon reaction with various CCDs concentrations in 1:1 ethanol-water. CCDs concentration of (a) 30 μg•mL−1, (b) 60 μg•mL−1, (c) 90 μg•mL−1. (d) Comparison of time dependence of UV−vis absorption for DPPH radical upon reaction with various CCDs concentrations.
(a) UV-vis absorbance for the SA-Fe2+-H2O2 aqueous solution upon gradual addition of CCDs from 0 to 1.2 mg•mL−1. (b) Active oxygen species scavenging activity of CCDs.

(a) The UV-vis curves for the BR/2CCDs and BR/2CCDs/5BR-g-MAH films (thickness of 0.6 mm). (b) The transparency for the rubber films: Sample Ⅰ refers to the BR/2CCDs and Sample Ⅱ refers to BR/2CCDs/5BR-g-MAH. (c) The photos of the rubber films before UV exposure. (d) The photos of the rubber films under UV exposure (365nm-wavelength).
SEM photos for the BR/2CCDs/5BR-g-MAH and BR/2CCDs samples. (the yellow circle indicates serious CCDs aggregation).
Curing curves of the rubber compounds.

The performances of the rubber composites before ageing, and ageing for 3 and 6 days. (a) The tensile strength. (b) The Elongation at break. (c) The aging coefficient. (d) The stress-strain curves of the NR, NR/4020-8, and NR/CCDs-8 composites before aging and aging for 6 days.
(a) The values of crosslinking density, (b) The change ratio of crosslinking density for rubber samples before aging and aging for various days.
(a) The DSC curves for the rubber composites. (b, c, d) The ATR curves for the rubber composites before aging and aging for 6 days.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Rubber ingredients: ZnO, 5 parts per hundred rubber (phr); stearic acid, 2 phr; accelerator CZ, 1.5 phr; DM, 0.5 phr; Sulfur 1.5.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The amount of CCDs fluid in the formula refers to the solid content in the CCDs fluid.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Curing characteristics of rubber compounds a .

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: T10: scorch time; T90: optimum cure time; ML: the minimum torque; MH: the maximum torque.

head.ce:title: The use of crude carbon dots as novel antioxidants for natural rubber

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Advanced Innovation Center for Soft Matter Science and Engineering, Center of Advanced Elastomer Materials, State Key Laboratory of Organic−Inorganic Composites, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing Advanced Innovation Center for Soft Matter Science and Engineering, Center of Advanced Elastomer Materials, State Key Laboratory of Organic−Inorganic Composites

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Beijing Advanced Innovation Center for Soft Matter Science and Engineering, Center of Advanced Elastomer Materials, State Key Laboratory of Organic−Inorganic Composites, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Natural rubber (NR) is acknowledged to be a strategically important material, which spans multiple applications from tires to shock absorbers, generally, the antioxidant is indispensable for NR to prolong the service life and improve the long-term reliability. Recent reports demonstrated that the purified carbon dots with the capability of scavenging oxy radicals can be effective antioxidants for rubbers. However, the application of purified CDs is greatly restricted by the multiple tedious, complicated, time-consuming, and expensive post-purification processes. Herein, we first synthesize the amine-passivated crude carbon dots (CCDs) by a facile microwave-assisted pyrolysis method, and we attempt to directly use the CCDs (without any purifications) as a novel and inexpensive antioxidant for NR. To achieve a fine CCDs dispersion within NR matrix, we disperse the CCDs into the NR matrix by the liquid feeding and in-situ compatibilizing. The CCDs fluid was firstly prepared by mixing the CCDs with water, then the CCDs fluid together with the compatibilizer was melt-compounded with NR. The results showed that the well-dispersed CCDs endowed NR with the impressive antioxidative property. Overall, this work extends application scope of CCDs and provides unique inspiration to develop novel and effective antioxidants for elastomers.

ce:section.ce:para: Natural rubber is one of the most important polymer materials, which has been widely used in the tires, seals, and shock absorbers. Nevertheless, the NR is plagued with aging issues in service. The abundant unsaturated C=C bonds in NR molecular chains makes it vulnerable to thermal/oxygen ageing, [1] which would result in a dramatic deterioration of the mechanical properties for NR composites. [2] Therefore, to retard the thermal-oxidative-aging process, the antioxidant is generally incorporated into the rubber composites. Unfortunately, most commercial rubber antioxidants are plagued with the toxicity issues, which might cause potential dangers and environmental pollution. Moreover, the industrialized processes for the production of most antioxidants are complicated and eco-unfriendly. [3] Apart from the traditional antioxidants, the macromolecular antioxidants have also been studied in recent years, [4-6] however, these antioxidants could not be easily scaled up and their complex synthesis procedures would cause more serious pollution. With environmental sustainability becoming an increasing concern, it is highly desirable to develop novel antioxidants with greener synthesis procedures and high anti-oxidative efficiency for rubbers.

Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted significant interest in the field of biological labeling, medical diagnosis, catalysis, and optoelectronics owing to the strong and tunable fluorescence emission, generally low cytotoxicity, biocompatibility, and facile synthetic routes from readily available substances. [7-9] Although CDs are well known for their florescence properties. Actually, many previous reports have already demonstrated that the carbon dots can be used as the antioxidants in various fields [10-15] due to their radical scavenging capability, especially, the purified amine-passivated CDs have already been demonstrated as tunable and effective antioxidants for elastomers. [3,16,17] Nevertheless, the purification process is complicated, energy- and time-consuming. [18] Generally, synthetic approaches for making CDs can roughly be lumped into two camps: top-down and bottom-up approaches. [19] In comparison with "top-down" synthetic route, the "bottom-up" route is the most promising route to afford low-cost and large-scale synthesis of CDs. [20] The well-accepted mechanism for CDs formation in the "bottom-up" route involves complicated ionization/polymerization of the precursors, which is followed by carbonization. [21] Therefore, the "bottom-up" route would inevitably produce the crude carbon dots (CCDs), i.e., a complex mixture of CDs, quasi CDs (organic molecular fluorophores attached to the core of CDs), polymer dots, and molecular fluorophores. In most studies, the by-products (such as quasi CDs, polymer dots, and molecular fluorophores) were removed from CCDs to obtain the pure CDs via post-treatments such as dialysis, filtration, centrifugation, column chromatography, and gel-electrophoresis. [22] However, these extensive procedures are expensive, time-consuming, and difficult to be scale-up. For instance, dialysis uses a semipermeable membrane that allows small molecules (molecular fluorophores) to pass through but stops larger structures such as carbon dots. This process generally takes several days to a week, and it would produce large amounts of wastewater, which apparently does not embrace the principles of green chemistry. Overall, current purification practices severely limit the broad applications of CDs materials.

Herein, we attempt to directly use the diethylenetriamine-passivated crude carbon dots (CCDs), without energy- and time-intensive purification process, as novel antioxidants for natural rubber. Considering the high hydrophilicity of CCDs, it is difficult to achieve a fine CCDs dispersion within hydrophobic NR matrix by traditional melt milling. We therefore use the combination of liquid feeding and in-situ compatibilizing, to prepare the NR/CCDs composites. In detail, the CCDs were firstly dispersed in water to form CCDs fluid. Then the CCDs fluid was liquid feed into the NR through melt-mixing, during which the coupling agent (BR-g-MAH) was added to improve the CCDs dispersion and NR-CCDs interfacial adhesion. [17] The effect of CCDs on the thermo-oxidative aging resistance of NR vulcanizate was systematically investigated by DSC analysis, accelerated thermal-oxidative aging tests, and FTIR-ATR analysis. Excitingly, the results show that the CCDs endowed the elastomer with impressive antioxidative property. We envisage that this scalable and environmentally-friendly strategy might provide novel inspiration to develop novel and effective antioxidants for the elastomers.

The NR (smoked sheet) was kindly donated by Tianjin Changli Rubber Trading Co., Ltd., China. BR-g-MAH (trade name of Ricobond 1731, the number averaged molecular weight of 5500, MAH content of 17 wt.%) was obtained from Cray Valley Company, France. Citric acid monohydrate was purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co. Ltd., China. Diethylenetriamine (DETA) was provided by Shanghai Aladdin biochemical technology Co., Ltd., China. Rubber additives were purchased from local sources. All chemical reagents were used as received without further refinement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CCD fluid and rubber composites

ce:section.ce:para: Amine-passivated CCDs were synthesized from citric acid monohydrate and DETA using a facile microwave-assisted synthesis method, in detail, 7.7 g of citric acid monohydrate was dissolved in 70 g KH2PO4 aqueous solution (0.002 mol/L), and then 7.56 g DETA was dropped slowly into the mixture solution under stirring condition. Subsequently, the homogeneous mixture solution was heated under 700 W microwave irradiation for 10 min in a domestic microwave oven. During the irradiation, the transparent liquid became a red-brown solid, indicating the formation of amine-passivated CCDs, following that, a desired amount of water was added into the system to obtain a homogeneous CCDs fluid with 60 wt% solid content.

The detail of processing is shown as follows. Firstly, a given amount of CCDs fluid with BR-g-MAH was well compounded with NR in an open two-roll mill at room temperature. To remove the water as much as possible, the NR/CCDs composites are subjected to an internal milling process (70 rpm at 120°C for 8 min) in an internal mixer. After that, the rubber additives were added into rubber compound by a two-roll mill following the conventional rubber processing procedure. For comparison, the NR/antioxidant 4020 composites were also prepared. The curing characteristics (e.g., maximum torque MH, minimum torque ML, scorch time T10, and optimum cure time T90) for the rubber samples were determined by a vulcameter. Lastly, the well-mixed rubber compounds were compression molded at 143°C for T90 + 3 min. The formulations of rubber composites are shown in Table 1 .
SEM investigations were conducted on a HITACHI SU-8020 SEM (HITACHI Co., Japan), the samples were plated with a thin gold layer before any observations.
Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) investigation was conducted on a JEM-3010 electron microscope (JEOL, Japan) with an acceleration voltage of 300 kV.
Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were measured on a Perkin Elmer GX spectrophotometer scanning from 4000∽400 cm−1 with a resolution of 4 cm−1.
X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) was investigated by using PHI-5300 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer with an Al Kα radiation source.

DPPH assay, the free radical scavenging activity of the CCDs were evaluated using a DPPH (2, 2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl free radical) assay previously described elsewhere, [16,23] in detail, the DPPH was firstly dissolved in the ethanol aqueous solution (50wt% ethanol) with concentration of 0.2028 mM. And the CCDs was dissolved in ethanol aqueous solution (50wt% ethanol) with various concentrations (0.27 mg mL−1, 0.18 mg mL−1, 0.09 mg mL−1). Subsequently, 1 ml CCDs solution was mixed with 2 ml DDPH solution. The radical scavenging activity was real-time measured by recording the loss of DPPH absorbance at 523 nm through UV-vis test scanning for 20 times continuously for 30 min (each scan takes 1.5 min, and 20 scans take 30 min).

The scavenging capacity of CCDs to active oxygen species generated by the Fenton reaction was investigated following the protocol reported elsewhere. [16] By detecting the CCDs-induced variation of the absorption peak around 525 nm for the salicylic acid/Fe (III) complex, the radical scavenging capacity of CCDs can be evaluated. The absorption spectra of the mixture solutions were obtained by a PerkinElmer Lambda 950 spectrophotometer.
The curing properties of the rubber compounds were investigated at 143°C by a Gotech RH-2000N vulkameter (Gotech Testing Machines Inc).

Hot-Air Ageing Test was carried out in an age-circulating oven at 100°C for 3 or 6 days according to GB/T3512-2001 standard. The mechanical properties of the dumb-bell shaped rubber composites were tested on a CMT4104 electronic tensile tester (SANS, China) with a strain rate of 500 mm/min according to ASTM D412. Tensile strength, Elongation at break, and aging coefficient (k) were calculated using the following equations:
(1)
Tensile strength retention
=
T a
/
T u
× 100 %
(2)
Elongation at break retention
=
E a
/
E u
× 100 %
(3)
Aging coefficient
=
(
T a
×
E a
)
/
(
T u
×
E u
)
× 100 %
Where Ta and Tu are the tensile strength of the aged and un-aged samples, respectively. Ea and Eu are the elongation at break of the aged and un-aged samples, respectively.

Crosslinking density was determined by the equilibrium swelling method (the toluene was used as solvent) according to the related reference. [24] The change ratio of crosslinking density was calculated according to the following equation:
(4)
The change ratio of crosslinking density
=
C a
/
C u
× 100 %
Where Ca and Cu are the crosslinking densities of aged and un-aged specimens, respectively.

Differential scanning calorimetry analysis (DSC) was performed on a DSC Q100 (TA Instruments, New Castle, DE). The sample was placed in an uncovered aluminum pan, it was firstly equilibrated at 30°C, then the sample was heated to 100°C with a rate of 20°C/min with N2 flow (50 mL/min). After equilibrating at 100°C for 3 min, the purge was switched to O2 flow (50 mL/min), and the sample was heated from 100°C to 280°C at a rate of 3°C/min, during which a sharp exothermic peak of the rubber oxidation can be observed.

In the FTIR spectrum of CCDs (Fig 1 a), the large, broad absorption band near 3400 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibrations of O-H and N-H groups, [25] and the band observed between 1640-1690 cm−1 could demonstrate the existence of C=O group. Also, the sharp band for N-H bending vibration of the amide group (1550-1640 cm−1) indicates the existence of –NH2 and amide groups on the surface of DETA-CCDs. [3,25,26] We further use the XPS analysis to investigate the DETA-CCDs, three types of N-related bonding can be identified in the deconvoluted N 1s spectrum (Fig 1c). The peaks at 399.6, 400.3, and 401.5 eV are assigned to the amine groups, amide-carbonyl groups, and graphitic N, respectively. The FTIR and XPS results clearly demonstrate the CCDs are functionalized with carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amine moieties, these groups account for the excellent water dispersibility of CCDs. Considering most of the commercial antioxidants contain the active hydroxyl, carboxyl and amine functionalities, the CCDs bearing these moieties might also possess the potential radical scavenging ability (will be discussed below). Fig. 1e illustrates the UV-Vis spectra for CCDs, the CCDs exhibit two absorption peaks around 240 and 340 nm, most probably attributing the π→π* and n→π* transitions of certain fluorophores in CCDs. [27] Fig. 1f shows that the CCDs fluoresce when exposed to UV light. The TEM was used to characterize the morphology of CCDs, in Fig. 1g, we could observe that many CCDs particles exhibit quasi-spherical shapes with an average size of about 3.4 nm. Also, the particles with a diameter of 20 nm could be observed (Fig. 1h), this is because the bottom-up synthesis of CDs from small molecular precursors generally involve rather complicated polymerization and carbonization reactions, [21] and it would inevitably produce CCDs, i.e., a complex mixture of molecular fluorophores, polymer dots, quasi CDs (organic molecular fluorophores attached to the core of CDs), CDs and large carbon particles, meaning that the CCDs are rather complicated system. Therefore, CCDs particles with various shapes and diameters can be observed. In Fig 1i, an obvious Tyndall effect for CCDs fluid (solid content of 60wt%) can be observed, revealing the uniform dispersion of CCDs in water.

We next use the DPPH assay to study the radical scavenging ability of CCDs (Fig. 2 ). The DPPH assay was widely used to investigate the free radical scavenging activity of antioxidants due to the hydrogen atom abstraction by the DPPH radicals. [28] The CCDs are effective scavengers because of the presence of large amounts of surface functional groups (-NH2, -CONH-, -OH, etc.) with proton-donating ability. We mixed the DPPH solution and CCDs solution with different concentrations, and the absorbance at 523 nm of the mixture was immediately recorded using an UV-vis spectrophotometer. All the mixed solutions exhibit a progressive decline in the absorption of DPPH radical at 523 nm (Fig 2a, 2b, and 2c), moreover, we also observe that the decline of UV-vis absorption at 523 nm were more pronounced for the mixed solutions with higher CCDs concentration (Fig 2d), demonstrating the DPPH radicals were effectively stabilized by the CCDs.

Furthermore, we use the Fenton reaction to evaluate the antioxidative-ability of CCDs. As noted before, the DETA-CCDs possess a large amount of surface moieties, including –OH, –NH2, and –CONH- groups. These groups with proton-donating ability could effectively quench the reactive radicals. [29] As shown in Fig. 3 a, we introduce CCDs with various concentrations from 0 to 1.2 mg/mL into the salicylic acid-Fe2+-H2O2 system. The H2O2 is decomposed into H2O and O2 under the catalyst of iron ion which cycles between Fe2+ and Fe3+. [17] Salicylic acid (SA) is introduced to detect Fe(III) because the Fe(III)/SA complex exhibits deep color and has obvious UV absorption around 525 nm in the UV–vis spectra. It is observed that the peak absorption around 525 nm decrease progressively when increasing CCDs concentration (Fig. 3a and 3b), demonstrating that the CCDs could effectively scavenge the reactive oxygen radicals and therefore reduces the content of Fe(III)/SA complex. Overall, the above results revealed that the CCDs can be used as potential antioxidants for polymers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dispersion of CCDs within the rubber
ce:section.ce:para: The above-mentioned evidences have clearly demonstrated that the DETA-CCDs exhibits superior antioxidative-ability. To prepare NR/CCDs composites with high antioxidative performance, it is also critical to achieve a homogeneous CCDs dispersion within NR matrix. To this end, the water was deliberately mixed with CCDs to form a CCDs fluid because the water molecules could effectively depress the interactions among CCDs particles, which is in favour of promoting the CCDs dispersion within NR matrix, meanwhile, the BR-g-MAH (compatibilizer) was introduced to interact/wrap the CCDs particles, thus preventing the fine CCDs particles from fusing again. [17] Also, the BR-g-MAH could effectively improve the interfacial adhesion between the NR matrix and CCDs filler, because the backbone of BR-g-MAH could co-vulcanize with the NR matrix while the anhydride groups of BR-g-MAH are ready to interact with the amine-passivated CCDs. [30]

When the CCDs loading is low, we found that it is difficult to observe the CCDs dispersion within NR vulcanizate (cured by sulfur packages) by SEM investigation because of the interference of the rubber additives (such as 5 phr ZnO). We therefore attempt to use the model rubber compounds to study the CCDs dispersion. The transparent BR was selected as the model rubber with DCP as the curing agent. The transparency of BR allowed us to use the light transmittance and fluorescent investigations to evaluate the CCDs dispersion within NR matrix. As shown in Fig. 4 , it is observed that the BR/2CCDs/5BR-g-MAH are more transparent compared to NR/2CCDs film (Fig 2a and Fig 2b). Transmittance ratios for BR/2CCDs/5BR-g-MAH and BR/2CCDs are 36% and 18.6% at the wavelength of 500 nm, respectively, indicating a better CCDs dispersion in BR/2CCDs/5BR-g-MAH composite than for BR/2CCDs composite. Moreover, when the rubber films were exposed under UV exposure, the BR/2CCDs film shows also most no fluorescent phenomena, in contrast, the BR/2CCDs/5BR-g-MAH film exhibit obvious fluorescent phenomena. Generally, CCDs aggregation would lead to a decrease of luminescence intensity even a luminescence quenching. [31] The fluorescent analysis clearly validates that the CCDs dispersion within rubber matrix is improved upon the BR-g-MAH addition.

In the BR model composites, as noted before, the DCP (1 phr) instead of sulfur packages were chosen as the curing agent, and the DCP would not cause disturbance in SEM investigation of CCDs within the rubber matrix. As shown in Fig. 5 , in the sample of BR/2CCDs, a unique micron-sized spherical CCDs aggregate could be clearly identified, this may be attributed to the fact that the CCDs nanoparticles have a strong tendency for self-association upon drying from CCDs fluid because the re-aggregation of filler is thermodynamically favorable. [32,33] In the sample of BR/2CCDs/5BR-gMAH, no serious CCDs aggregate can be observed. All of this demonstrates the BR-g-MAH could effectively improve the CCDs dispersion within the rubber matrix.

We studied the effect of CCDs and 4020 additions on the curing behavior of NR rubber. As shown in Fig. 6 and Table 2 , the T90 for NR, NR/4020-2, NR/4020-4 and NR/4020-8 composites were 19:31, 17:15, 14:24, and 10:31 min, respectively, suggesting that the vulcanization process of NR was accelerated by the antioxidant. [34] Also, the ΔM for NR/4020 composites decreases when increasing the loading of antioxidant 4020, which is most probably because antioxidant 4020 is a small molecule compound, which acts as a plasticizer and therefore decrease the torque. [23] Similar phenomena were observed in the NR/CCDs composites. The incorporation of CCDs could decrease the T90 of the rubber composites, this is because the CCDs contains large amounts of amine groups, which could catalyze the formation of active sulfurating agents that is necessary for curing of rubber and therefore accelerates rubber vulcanization. [35] The CCDs addition decreases the MH of the rubber compounds because the CCDs contain large amounts of small molecules (fluorescent molecules).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Aging performance of the NR composite
ce:section.ce:para: Thermal-oxidative aging is the most common form of rubber aging, because the rubber composites are generally used in air. The thermo-oxidative aging could significantly deteriorate the mechanical performances of the NR composites. The accelerated thermal aging test was carried out to evaluate the thermo-oxidative aging resistance of the rubber composites. The changes of tensile strength retention and the elongation at break retention for the rubber composites ageing for 3 and 6 days are illustrated in Fig. 7 a and b, respectively. It is obvious that the NR without any antioxidant is easily suffered from thermal-oxidative aging. The elongation at break for the NR decreased drastically with increasing ageing time, for instance, the NR sample lost most of its elongation at break after 6 days. In contrast, the NR containing antioxidant 4020 or CCDs exhibit substantially improved anti-aging performances. And the anti-oxidative efficiency of CCDs is even superior than for commercial antioxidant 4020. We further used the ageing coefficient (k) to comprehensively evaluate the anti-ageing performance of the elastomers (Fig. 7c), similar results were observed. i.e., the CCDs can protect the NR composites more effectively compared to the widely used antioxidant 4020 in rubber industry. Fig. 7d shows that the tensile curves of NR, NR/4020-8 and NR/CCDs-8 before and after aging for 6 days. Before aging, it is observed that adding CCDs have no adverse effects on the mechanical properties, even improve the mechanical performance of the rubber composites. After aging for 6 days, the NR almost lost its mechanical strength. Compared to the antioxidant 4020, the incorporation of CCDs could more effectively retard the thermal oxidation aging of the NR vulcanizate. Overall, accelerated thermal aging tests showed that the CCDs could be effective antioxidants for the elastomer. This is because the CCDs could effectively scavenge the oxygen oxy radicals, thus terminate the free radical chain reaction of oxidative process.

The thermal-oxidative aging will cause both the degradation and crosslinking of the NR macromolecular chains. And the cross-linking of the rubber chains generally dominates the thermal aging process. Therefore, the thermal oxidative aging would increase the crosslink density of the rubber composites (Fig. 8 a). As shown in Fig. 8b, the change ratio of the crosslinking density for neat NR is higher than for NR/4020 and NR/CCDs composites. Indicating that both the antioxidant 4020 and CCDs could slow down the rubber chain oxidation. Also, it seems that the change ratio for NR/CCDs composites is lower than for NR/4020 composites, suggesting that the anti-oxidative ability of CCDs is superior compared to antioxidant 4020. The results of crosslinking density determination agreed well with the mechanical performance results.

Furthermore, we select the NR, NR/4020-4, and NR/CCDs-4 samples to have the DSC testing, as shown in Fig. 9 a, these samples were heated from 100°C to 280°C under O2 atmosphere, the samples exhibit an obvious oxidation exothermic peak. Meanwhile, the oxidation exothermic peak of the NR/CCDs-4 and NR/4020-4 is gentle and smooth while the peak of neat NR is sharp, indicating that the oxidation reaction of NR/CCDs-4 and NR/4020-4 is not severe as that of NR. Moreover, the enthalpy of oxidation for the rubber samples is in the order of NR/CCDs-4 (437 J/g), NR/4020-4 (561 J/g), and NR (819 J/g). Meaning that both the CCDs and antioxidant 4020 could improve the anti-oxidative property of the rubber composites, and CCDs is more effective.

We further use the FTIR-ATR spectroscopy to study the internal structure changes of the rubber composites, we can observe that the absorption bands for (C=O)-O- (3552 cm−1), OH (3420 cm−1), and C=O (1735 cm−1) increased with the aging time, and this phenomenon is more pronounced in the NR sample without antioxidant. The appearance of these groups indicates the generation of carbonyl compounds, such as ester, ketone, etc., demonstrating there is an oxidation process for natural rubber. We can also observe that the absorption band of CH2 (2848 cm−1) is almost unchanged, and the ratio of the absorbance of the C=O to that of CH2 (A(C=O)/A(CH2)) can be used to quantitatively determine the thermo-oxidative degree of the rubber compounds. The relative intensities of (A(C=O)/A(CH2)) for the rubber samples after aging for 6 days are in the order of NR>NR/4020>NR/CCDs, demonstrating both the antioxidant 4020 and CCDs could retard the oxidation of the NR, and the CCDs display a superior antioxidative efficiency, this result is consistent with the conclusions drawn from the above accelerated aging test.

To sum, we have demonstrated that the CCDs, without any lengthy, cumbersome, and costly purifications, can be directly used as effective antioxidants for NR. We first characterize the CCDs, notably, the DPPH test and Fenton reaction both demonstrate the CCDs could effectively scavenge the oxy radicals. To achieve a fine dispersion of CCDs within the matrix, we use the liquid feeding technique in presence of compatibilizer to prepare the NR/CCDs composites. The antioxidative performance of CCDs in NR vulcanizates was investigated by FTIR-ATR spectroscopy, DSC analysis, crosslinking density determination, and mechanical properties testing. The results revealed that the CCDs could significantly suppress the oxidation process of NR. Considering the easy fabrication of CCDs that without any time- and energy- intensive post purification processes, we envision that the CCDs are potential to be a novel type of rubber antioxidant with high performance, green and inexpensive characteristics, which might broaden the scope of CCDs application and provide novel inspiration to prepare the novel antioxidants with economic and simple synthetic routs for a greener sustainable future.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51790501), the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2017YFB0306903), and National Natural Science Foundation of China (51988102, 51503010).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Renewable conjugated acids as curatives for high-performance rubber/silica composites
Green Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Lin, X. Zhang, Z. Tang, B. Guo, Renewable conjugated acids as curatives for high-performance rubber/silica composites, Green Chem.17(6) (2015) 3301-3305.
